title: SACRAMENTO –
prompt_preview:

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Reducing speed limits in school zones brings significant safety benefits.",
    "Lowering speed limits alone isn’t enough for safety in school zones.",
    "California has school zone policies that are confusing for drivers and hard for public safety officials to enforce.",
    "Traffic violence is the number one killer of children in California.",
    "AB 2583 aims to create better safety standards for children in school zones.",
    "California is one of just nine states with a school zone speed limit greater than 20 mph.",
    "Only six other states mandate the use of 'when children are present' signs for school zones.",
    "A time-based standard for school zone speed limits is easier for drivers to comply with.",
    "The average risk of severe injury for a pedestrian struck by a vehicle increases significantly with speed.",
    "At an impact speed of 16 mph, the risk of severe injury for a pedestrian is 10% and increases to 25% at 23 mph.",
    "The risk of death for pedestrians increases from 4% at 15 mph to 12% at 25 mph.",
    "One out of 10 pedestrians struck by a vehicle traveling at 40 mph will survive.",
    "Nine out of 10 pedestrians will survive when a vehicle is traveling at 20 mph.",
    "Research shows that up to 72% of children injured as pedestrians will have functional limitations six months after their injury.",
    "Lower speeds allow drivers to better see their surroundings and react to potential hazards.",
    "Lower speed limits reduce the likelihood of unsafe driver behavior in school zones.",
    "Distracted driving is more likely during afternoon pick-up times in school zones where the speed limit is more than 20 mph."
  ]
}
```
